summary: The testing strategy covers the main happy-path scenarios but contains three TDD anchors that describe substring/presence checks rather than behavioral assertions, and is missing coverage for three acceptance criteria (error handling path, non-Windows no-op, ProxyState backward compatibility). No critical issues, but the pattern of 'contains X in response' integration tests and 'override present in JS' unit tests will produce tests that verify structure rather than behavior.

findings.location: Section: Testing Strategy > Unit tests > navigator.permissions.query override
findings.issue: Anchor reads 'navigator.permissions.query override present in injected JS' — this describes a substring presence check on the JS text, not a behavioral assertion. A test that passes when the string 'permissions.query' appears in the injection output would still pass even if the override was syntactically broken or returned the wrong state.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite anchor as: 'Test that inject.js navigator.permissions.query override — when evaluated in a JS context with a stub navigator — returns {state: "granted"} for name="geolocation" and delegates to a real stub for other permission names.' Assertions: (1) parsed JS executes without error, (2) calling the override with {name:"geolocation"} returns a Promise resolving to {state:"granted"}, (3) calling with {name:"camera"} delegates to original handler.

findings.location: Section: Testing Strategy > Integration tests > item 1
findings.issue: Anchor reads 'response contains geolocation override JS (but not timezone/language)' — both clauses are substring presence assertions. This does not verify that the injected JS actually overrides navigator.geolocation; it only verifies that certain text appears in the HTTP response body. A broken injection that includes the right keywords but is syntactically invalid would still pass.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite as two behavioral assertions: (1) the injected script block, when extracted and evaluated in a JS sandbox, overrides navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition to invoke the callback with fake coordinates; (2) the injected script does NOT contain timezone or language override assignments (this second clause can remain a negative substring check since it verifies absence of unintended content). If a full JS sandbox evaluation is impractical in integration tests, at minimum assert that the response body contains a syntactically valid <script> tag and that no 'Intl' or 'language' override tokens are present.

findings.location: Section: Testing Strategy > Integration tests > item 3
findings.issue: Anchor reads 'response contains full JS payload (regression)' — 'contains' implies a substring check. This regression test only verifies text presence, not that the full payload (timezone, language, geolocation, WebRTC, permissions) is functionally complete. A payload missing the WebRTC override would still contain the keyword and pass.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite as: 'Test that target domain HTML response includes a script block covering all five override categories: geolocation, timezone (Intl), language (navigator.language/languages), WebRTC (RTCPeerConnection), and permissions.query.' Assertions should verify presence of each category's override token (five explicit checks) rather than a single 'full payload' substring. This makes the regression surface explicit.

findings.location: Section: Testing Strategy — acceptance criterion: request() error handling
findings.issue: Acceptance criteria explicitly requires: 'request() error handling: try/except around intercept, log on failure, pass through'. No TDD anchor covers this path. If the intercept raises an exception, the test suite provides no protection against a regression that crashes the request flow instead of passing through.
findings.recommendation: Add unit test anchor: 'Test that when the geolocation API intercept raises an exception, the request is passed through unchanged and the error is logged.' Approach: mock the response-fabrication call to raise an exception; assert (1) the request flow method returns without re-raising, (2) the logger receives an error/warning call, (3) the request object is unmodified (no response was set on it).

findings.location: Section: Testing Strategy — acceptance criterion: non-Windows no-op
findings.issue: Acceptance criteria requires 'Non-Windows platforms: Location Services functions are no-ops'. No TDD anchor covers this. The functions use winreg which is Windows-only; tests running on Linux/Mac CI would fail or skip silently without an explicit no-op test.
findings.recommendation: Add unit test anchor: 'Test that disable_location_services() and restore_location_services() return without error and make no winreg calls when platform is not Windows.' Approach: mock sys.platform to 'linux'; assert winreg is never imported or called; assert function returns None without exception.

findings.location: Section: Testing Strategy — acceptance criterion: ProxyState backward compatibility
findings.issue: Tech-spec decision 4 states 'existing state files remain loadable (backward compatible)' for the new original_location_services field. No TDD anchor covers deserialization of a state file that lacks this field. A missing default or incorrect field typing could break startup when loading pre-feature state files.
findings.recommendation: Add unit test anchor: 'Test that ProxyState can be deserialized from a JSON/dict that does not contain original_location_services, and that the field defaults to None.' Assertions: (1) instantiation from old-format dict succeeds, (2) original_location_services is None, (3) existing fields are unaffected.

findings.location: Section: Testing Strategy > Unit tests > disable_location_services / restore_location_services
findings.issue: Anchor says 'mock winreg, verify correct key/value operations' — the phrase 'correct key/value operations' is ambiguous. It could mean only asserting that winreg.SetValueEx was called (mock-only pattern) rather than verifying the preservation logic: that the value read before disable is the exact value written back by restore.
findings.recommendation: Clarify anchor to: 'Test that restore_location_services() writes back the exact value previously read by disable_location_services(), not a hardcoded Allow.' Assertions: (1) mock winreg to return 'Deny' on read; (2) call disable then restore; (3) assert SetValueEx was called with 'Deny', not 'Allow'; (4) separately test the None case: if key did not exist, restore deletes the key rather than setting a value.
